title: 根据一项新调查，驱动 AI 投资回报的 7 个因素
author: Thomas H. Davenport, Laks Srinivasan
content:
Organizations are spending significant dollars on AI. According to one estimate, U.S. companies spent $37 billion in 2025 for generative AI alone. Increasingly, senior executives and boards are asking about returns and there are looming consequences for leaders that don’t have good answers. Seventy-one percent of global chief information officers in a recent survey said that their AI budgets would be frozen or cut if value from AI couldn’t be demonstrated within two years.

We wanted a closer look at how companies are faring in this effort—and where they think they’re headed in the near future. So, we undertook a research study (sponsored by Scaled Agile’s AI training business) in late 2025 and early 2026 on companies’ perceptions of value from AI and how they are achieving it. This included interviews with 12 technology, data, and AI leaders at enterprise organizations and a survey of 1,006 global senior executives who had close familiarity with their organizations’ AI initiatives.

The results were surprisingly positive: 45% of respondents said they are achieving a great deal of value from AI and an additional 45% said they are achieving moderate value. Only 9% indicated that their organization is achieving a small amount of value, and virtually zero (0.2%) said they were achieving no value. This relatively high level of reported value runs counter to some widely publicized studies suggesting much lower returns, although they were focused on value from generative AI alone.

However, how companies achieve value may be the real surprise. We found that there are at least seven factors that can drive economic value from AI. Moreover, several of the value levers can be combined into an economic maturity model for AI that can drive substantial value.

Seven Factors that Drive High AI Value

Let’s start with what isn’t driving value. (At least not yet.) Headcount reduction, for instance, is not yet a meaningful driver; in a previous article we reported that only 2% of announced reductions or slowed hiring are actually enabled by existing AI capabilities in production. The rest is anticipatory benefit from AI or “AI washing” of reductions made for other reasons. Generative AI is also not yet yielding a high level of value for businesses. Creating chief AI officer (or chief data and AI officer) roles also isn’t driving value creation.

Your Leader Profile

What’s Your Leadership Superpower?

Take this quiz to discover your superpower and unlock Your Leader Profile.

Start Here!

According to the survey respondents and enterprise technology leaders, across seven factors the data point in one direction: Organizations getting the most from AI are not necessarily those with the most sophisticated technology. What separates them is more fundamental: how clearly leaders define what value means for their organization, who they hold accountable for delivering it, and how seriously they treat measurement not as a reporting exercise but as a management discipline.

Here’s what seems to be working at the high-value companies:

1. Be clear on what type of value you are trying to achieve.

The great majority of respondents to the survey say they are getting value from AI. How they define that value, however, can differ quite a bit. For example, 14% of respondents report receiving a great deal of value from AI, but only slight return on investment in the technology. Similarly, 9% report moderate value but substantial ROI.

What’s going on here? Put simply: Value is in the eye of the beholder. In these responses, value should be understood as “AI is doing what we want it to do.”

Some organizations strive for short-term ROI, others for long-term transformation. Prem Natarajan, executive VP and head of enterprise AI, data, and staff technologies at Capital One, commented on why the bank isn’t interested in pursuing only short-term returns:

A focus on short-term value is why many enterprises never make the technology transformation that unlocks long-term value. Only the companies built on a modern tech stack and deeply invested in proprietary data will be in a position to transform their business by putting AI at the center of it.

Other organizations, however, may be focused on more immediate returns on their AI investments. But in either case, leaders need to be clear on what they’re trying to achieve and why.

2. Seek value in both products and processes—even if the payoff isn’t immediate.

Most organizations focus on AI value from internal process improvements. Several of the executives we interviewed, however, were equally or more focused on AI in customer offerings.

One was Laura Matz, chief science and technology officer at Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany, which operates across life science, healthcare, and electronics. She said that the diverse company has both process-oriented and product-focused AI initiatives underway. Most of the products or new businesses that incorporate AI are relatively new and in the pilot stage, so it is difficult to value them until they gain additional scale. The new AI-enabled offerings may require new ecosystems, new business models, or new technologies to be developed, but after more than 350 years in business, Merck KGaA has a long-term perspective on innovation.

Philippe Rambach, chief AI officer at Schneider Electric, the French energy technology multinational, described a deliberate dual focus. Internal AI applications deliver more immediate financial returns, helping employees work faster and better while providing enhanced support for customers. Customer-facing AI at Schneider represents a longer-term strategic play focused on capturing market share in evolving markets. Each requires different approaches to measuring success and different timelines for realizing value.

AI can also be understood as defending a company’s value. Julien Sauvagnargues, president of Olympus Corporation of Americas, whose medical endoscopes use AI to identify potentially cancerous polyps and reduce the administrative burden of documentation, noted that companies need to think about AI offerings as a way to protect market share, and not just as pathways to new value:

AI is appealing in the marketplace. If we don’t have it in our products we will lose market share. We know what the AI will cost us and how much the cost of not doing it is.

Olympus also uses AI for personal productivity objectives but does not measure the productivity benefits yet.

3. Use all the tools in the AI toolbox.

Generative AI dominates media coverage, but it’s not what most organizations find most valuable. Among our survey respondents, 50% said their companies get the most value from analytical AI, such as dynamic pricing or customer targeting. Rule-based AI, often found in anti-money-laundering systems, insurance underwriting, and healthcare clinical decision support, as well as in robotic process automation was a close second; 40% of respondents said these tools produced the most value. Only 9% choose generative AI, and just 2% agentic AI, though these have, of course, only been in wide use for a few years.

That said, agentic adoption is an indicator of AI value; adopters are 22% more likely to report achieving a great deal of value from AI overall than non-adopters, and more likely to be employing relatively mature economic value practices.

4. Adopt a framework or method for achieving value.

Whether custom-built or drawn from the management literature, a structured approach to moving AI from idea to production to measured value is often essential for creating value. Ally Financial, the American bank holding company, has a custom “AI playbook” that guides its business lines from use-case exploration through responsible production deployment. In another case, an electrical utility uses the “stage gate” approach—more common in R&D—to manage the same journey.

The most common approach among our interview subjects, however, is a digital product focus, which manages both internal and external AI offerings as products from conception to implementation and ongoing use.

Shamin Mohamed, the EVP and chief information and technology officer of CarMax, the U.S. used-car retailer, credited a product orientation as the single most important factor in achieving value: “It brings a structure for proposing benefit, reviewing it over time, changing the business, and having stakeholders take accountability for value achieved.”

Any value framework must also include a means of getting your data ready for AI: 55% cited unready data as an impediment to value, which is consistent with other surveys.

5. Involve the CFO and the finance function in achieving and certifying value.

Most organizations assign AI value accountability to chief data/analytics/AI officer (38%) or individual functional executives (35%). Only 2% assign it to the CFO, but when they are responsible for achieving AI value, 76% of organizations in our survey reported that they achieved a “great deal” of value. That compares to 53% under CIOs or CTOs, and only 32% under functional executives. The finance function brings rigor, credibility, and organizational authority that other roles often lack.

Several companies we interviewed have finance organizations partnering with technology executives to certify value from AI. Nimish Panchmatia, who oversees AI for Singapore-based DBS Bank, described their approach:

Since 2021, the bank has reported the economic value delivered by data analytics and AI in its Annual Report. They track outcomes from A/B testing, quantify the difference as economic value, and each unit’s CFO validates their respective figures, which then roll up to an aggregate group-level number.

6. Train both users and executives on AI.

There is a two-tier challenge: 58% of organizations haven’t trained employees in AI productivity and tool use, while 29% acknowledge leaders lack the understanding to drive AI value creation. Organizations that invest in both employee upskilling and leadership AI fluency see a 23-percentage-point advantage in value realization.

Critically, employee adoption isn’t the barrier—only 13% cite workforce resistance as an inhibitor to AI value. Employees aren’t resisting, but rather waiting for effective senior leadership and the removal of other barriers like missing value frameworks and unready data.

7. Follow an AI economic value maturity model.

These models predict meaningfully different levels of value achievement. This economic maturity models is based on three components.

The first is simply to put AI systems into production—pilots and experiments can be useful for learning but yield no economic value. The more production use cases the better; only 6% say they currently have just one or more pilot projects but no production use cases, while 58% say they have one or a few production use cases, and 36% have many use cases in production using all types of AI.

A second component of the maturity model is to assess the value of production use cases, ideally both before and after implementation. In our survey a near-majority assesses value before and after implementation, but just for some use cases. Only 11% do that for all use cases. More companies—32%—assess value only before implementation, and 9% don’t have enough use cases in production to bother with assessing value.

The third component of the maturity model is to aggregate value across the organization and report it, at least informally. Surprisingly, a majority (72%) of organizations report that they aggregate the value of AI use cases. The final component is more formal reporting—in some cases externally.

An AI Economic Maturity Model

Based on these components, we developed a model for predicting how AI investment might provide additional economic value. We identified six stages, each of which is a step up in terms of both sophistication and predicted ROI. To develop this, we analyzed responses to our survey and looked at where respondents said their companies were in their AI journey and whether their organizations had achieved a “great deal of value.” Here’s how it works:

Stage 0: Only Unmeasured Pilots (3% of surveyed organizations)

Organizations run AI experiments but don’t measure outcomes. Only 4% of the respondents in this stage achieved a “great deal.”

Stage 1: Production Without Assessment (11%)

In this stage, the organization has moved beyond pilots and has deployed AI in production—that is, they’re using it in actual business practices—but aren’t evaluating the business impact it’s having. We found that simply moving past the pilot phase produces a leap forward in ROI—18% of respondents in this stage reported a “great deal” of value from their AI investments.

Stage 2: Pre-Implementation Assessment (17%)

In this stage, organizations take a slightly more structure approach to thinking about the business impacts of AI implementation, but only on the front end of the process: They justify AI projects with ROI projections and business cases, but still don’t validate outcomes. This step forward produces only a modest improvement over the previous stage, increasing to 20% those who say they’re achieving a “great deal” of value. This is the smallest degree of added value from a stage transition in the model.

Stage 3: Post-Implementation Assessment (30%)

Here, organizations measure individual AI use cases after deployment, an step that produces a surge in value: 44% of respondents in this stage reported that they were achieving a “great deal” of value from AI—more than double than Stage 2.

As such, we identified this as the first major inflection point in the AI adoption journey. But we also found that companies seem to get stuck here. Respondents said they spent a median of six years in this stage, making it something of a bottleneck.

Stage 4: Aggregated Annual Assessment (21%)

In this stage, organizations roll up AI value across the portfolio annually, convert benefits into an aggregated level of value for the organization, and make the results available informally throughout the firm. This allows leader to compare total value from AI against total expenditures on AI and can also enable comparisons to revenue and profit growth or decline.

This more formalized process creates a significant step up in value creation: 58% of respondents in this stage reported achieving a “great deal” of value from AI.

Stage 5: Formal Reporting (16%)

In our final stage, organizations report AI value to boards, investors, or public markets, which requires a high level of rigor and accountability of AI value measurement. Here, value surges again: 85% of respondents report achieving a “great deal” of value from AI—the second and largest inflection point.

Some executives expressed caution about external reporting. One noted concern that analysts might respond by pressuring the company to increase its dividend if strong AI returns were publicly disclosed. But the executives we interviewed generally felt that formal external reporting represents the highest level of AI economic maturity when it can be achieved responsibly.

The path to AI value is not primarily a technical challenge—it is a management one. Every organization now has access to AI technology, but only some will deploy it in ways that generate real and measurable economic returns. These seven factors, and especially the maturity model, offer a practical roadmap for becoming one of them.
aiSummary: 这份 HBR 报告识别了七个管理驱动因素和一个六阶段成熟度模型，使组织能够从 AI 实验阶段过渡到可衡量的经济价值。
oneSentenceSummary: 这份 HBR 报告识别了七个管理驱动因素和一个六阶段成熟度模型，使组织能够从 AI 实验阶段过渡到可衡量的经济价值。
summary: 根据对 1，000 多名全球高管的调查，本文挑战了 AI 回报率低的说法，揭示了 90% 的组织正在看到中等到高的价值。核心发现是 AI 的成功是一种管理学科，而非技术学科。关键驱动因素包括定义清晰的价值类型、平衡流程 AI 和产品 AI，以及利用完整的“AI 工具箱”——其中分析型 AI 在价值创造方面目前优于生成式 AI。至关重要的是，研究强调让 CFO 参与价值认证会带来显著更高的成功率。文章最后提出了一个“AI 经济价值成熟度模型”，描绘了从未经衡量的试点项目到正式外部报告的历程，并将实施后评估和正式报告确定为 ROI 激增的两个主要拐点。

mainPoints.point: AI 价值实现主要是一个管理挑战，而非技术挑战。
mainPoints.explanation: 成功取决于领导者如何清晰地定义价值、谁来承担责任，以及衡量是否被视为严谨的管理学科，而非仅仅是报告工作。

mainPoints.point: 分析型和基于规则的 AI 目前比生成式 AI 带来更多的商业价值。
mainPoints.explanation: 尽管生成式 AI 占据头条新闻，但 50% 的高价值组织从分析型 AI（例如，动态定价）中获得最大收益，而只有 9% 的组织将生成式 AI 列为主要价值驱动因素。

mainPoints.point: CFO 的参与是实现和认证 AI 价值的关键催化剂。
mainPoints.explanation: CFO 负责 AI 价值的组织报告在实现“巨大价值”方面有 76% 的成功率，显著高于由 CIO（53%）或职能部门负责人（32%）领导的组织。

mainPoints.point: AI 经济价值成熟度模型识别出两个主要的 ROI 拐点。
mainPoints.explanation: 当进入实施后评估（第三阶段）时，价值显著激增，并在正式外部报告（第五阶段）期间达到顶峰，这要求最高水平的严谨性。

mainPoints.point: 以产品为中心的方法优于基于项目的 AI 实施。
mainPoints.explanation: 将 AI 计划作为持续的数字产品进行管理，确保了提出收益、持续审查和明确的利益相关者责任的结构，以实现长期价值。

keyQuotes: AI 价值之路主要不是一个技术挑战，而是一个管理挑战。
从 AI 中获益最多的组织，不一定是拥有最复杂技术的组织。
价值在于旁观者。在这些回应中，价值应被理解为“AI 正在做我们希望它做的事情”。
只有 9% 的人选择生成式 AI，而只有 2% 的人选择智能体 AI [作为产生最大价值的]，尽管这些技术仅广泛使用了几年。
财务职能带来了其他角色通常缺乏的严谨性、可信度和组织权威。
